death.cause: childbirth complications (died one month after daughter born Sept 3, 1709)

narrative_blocks.header: Born after her father died
narrative_blocks.body: Her father William Hooker died in 1689; she was born June 8, 1690, after his death. Her mother Susanna Fenn Blackleach died around 1698 — leaving Susannah an orphan at approximately eight years old. She was raised in the Farmington household through the end of the seventeenth century.

narrative_blocks.header: Died in childbirth at nineteen
narrative_blocks.body: Her daughter Susannah was born on September 3, 1709. On October 5, 1709 — barely one month later — she died in Stamford at age 19. The cause is almost certainly childbirth complications.

death.cause: childbirth complications (died one month after daughter born Sept 3, 1709)

narrative_blocks.body: Her father William Hooker died in 1689; she was born June 8, 1690, after his death. Her mother Susanna Fenn Blackleach died around 1698 — leaving Susannah an orphan at approximately eight years old. She was raised in the Farmington household through the end of the seventeenth century.

narrative_blocks.body: Her daughter Susannah was born on September 3, 1709. On October 5, 1709 — barely one month later — she died in Stamford at age 19. The cause is almost certainly childbirth complications.

burial.plot_notes: East Norwalk Historical Cemetery, Norwalk, Fairfield, Connecticut

H00253.gravestone_inscription: Susanna St. John, ye wife of Capt. Joseph St. John died December ye 4th, 1749 aged 40 years and 2 months. "She that lies at rest within this tomb / Had Rachel's face and Leah's fruitful womb, / Abigail's wisdom, Lydia's faithful heart / With Martha's care — we hope Mary's better part."

enslaved_persons.name: Jenny
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved woman. Listed in estate inventory of Joseph St. John, Dec 7, 1756.

enslaved_persons.name: London
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved man. Listed in estate inventory of Joseph St. John, Dec 7, 1756.

enslaved_persons.name: Peter
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved boy. Listed in estate inventory of Joseph St. John, Dec 7, 1756.

narrative_blocks.header: Her mother died one month after her birth
narrative_blocks.body: She was born September 3, 1709 in Norwalk. Her mother Hannah Hooker died October 5, 1709 — barely one month later, age nineteen, of complications from childbirth.

narrative_blocks.header: Married 'The Blue Sheep' captain in 1729
narrative_blocks.body: She married Capt. Joseph St. John in 1729 — known as 'The Blue Sheep' for the local tradition that he once dyed a sheep blue, though the rains washed it out. He served as Norwalk selectman seven times across thirty years.

narrative_blocks.header: Three generations of slaveholding descendants
narrative_blocks.body: After her death, her husband's 1756 estate inventory listed three enslaved people: Jenny, London, and Peter. Three consecutive generations of her descendants held enslaved people — including grandson William St. John, who sent his enslaved man Dover to the Continental Army in his own place.

narrative_blocks.header: Earned 'Blue Sheep' nickname by dyeing his flock
narrative_blocks.body: Tradition holds that Capt. Joseph Saint John attempted to dye a sheep blue to create a matching flock — and the rains washed it out, leaving him with the lasting nickname 'The Blue Sheep.' He was a large landowner in Norwalk and among its founding civic leaders, serving as Selectman, Rate Constable, Fence Viewer, and Captain of the 1st Company of the Norwalk trainband (1748).

narrative_blocks.header: Wife Susanna's epitaph: Rachel's face, Leah's fruitful womb
narrative_blocks.body: When Susanna Selleck Saint John died in December 1749 at forty, Capt. Joseph commissioned a tombstone epitaph comparing her to Rachel ('face'), Leah ('fruitful womb'), Abigail ('wisdom'), Lydia ('faithful heart'), and Martha ('care'). The epitaph — one of the most elaborate in the East Norwalk churchyard — captures how colonial Norwalk elites memorialized women through accumulated biblical virtues.

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Joseph St. John of Norwalk

narrative_blocks.header: Sarah died young
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah died in 1741 at the age of seven.

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Joseph St. John of Norwalk

enslaved_persons.name: Peter
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved man. Listed in 1786 estate inventory.

enslaved_persons.name: Phyllis
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved woman. Listed in 1786 estate inventory.

military_service.notes: Capt. 1st Co. Norwalk May 1761; Major 9th Regt. May 1775; Major 1st Batt. Col. Whiting Nov 1776; Lt. Col. 9th Regt. May 1777. On committee of Articles of Confederation 1777.

bio.title: Lieutenant Colonel
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Lt. Col. Stephen Saint John Esq.

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave #44152495. Stone: 'Lt Col command of the 9th Reg of Foot Guard of the State of Connecticut who departed this life May the 9th 1785, in the 50th year of his age.'

narrative_blocks.header: Led Connecticut's 9th Regiment of Foot as Lt.
narrative_blocks.body: Stephen Saint John rose to Lieutenant Colonel in command of the 9th Regiment of Foot, Guard of the State of Connecticut — a rank inscribed on his tombstone in East Norwalk Historical Cemetery. His family's marriage alliances connected two of Norwalk's most prominent dynasties: the Saint Johns married into the Fitches across two consecutive generations.

narrative_blocks.header: He sent no substitute; his brother did
narrative_blocks.body: A Connecticut selectman and Revolutionary officer, he led the colony’s 9th Regiment of Foot. When the war came, an enslaved man named Dover was sent to fight in the Continental Army in place of his brother — while his own household still held two people, Peter and Phyllis, at his death.

parents.father_name: Capt. Joseph St. John of Norwalk
parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Joseph St. John of Norwalk CT; d. 1756. His estate included enslaved people.

narrative_blocks.header: Orphaned at six; married the town's future captain
narrative_blocks.body: Her mother Susannah Selleck died in December 1749 when Susannah was barely six, and her father Capt. Joseph St. John died in 1756 when she was thirteen, leaving her to grow up in Norwalk without either parent. She married Captain Eliphalet Lockwood at thirty-three — he would go on to lose his home to British torches and organize the city's first volunteer fire department.

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Joseph St. John of Norwalk
Capt. Joseph St. John of Norwalk

enslaved_persons.name: Dover
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved man, jointly owned with brother Stephen. On June 3, 1777, William sent Dover to fight in the Continental Army in his stead, receiving half-ownership of Dover as credit from Stephen. "Received of my brother Stephen St. John, the one half of his Negro boy named Dover, now inlisted into the Continental Service, for the one half of which boy I am obliged to pay sixty dollars on demand, to exempt me from all actual service. William St. John." William was a Loyalist; his wife's 1800 census shows 1 enslaved person still in household.

education.notes: A.B. 1766, A.M. 1769. Same class as Jared Ingersoll and Jonathan Ingersoll.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale A.B. 1766, A.M. 1769 — same class as Hons. Jared and Jonathan Ingersoll of Connecticut.

burial.plot_notes: East Norwalk Historical Cemetery. FA confirmation.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale class of 1766
narrative_blocks.body: William Saint John graduated Yale with A.B. 1766 and A.M. 1769, in the same class as Hon. Jared and Jonathan Ingersoll — two of Connecticut's most prominent legal and political figures — and served as private tutor in the wealthy Phillipse family of Phillipse, New York. He was described by contemporaries as 'a very fine looking, superior man, a person of fine address and manners, presiding handsomely at his own table.'

narrative_blocks.header: Two verdicts: DAR Patriot Ancestor and colonial Loyalist
narrative_blocks.body: One chronicler records that Saint John 'was a Loyalist and was sent to New York with the Army,' yet he is simultaneously honored as a DAR Patriot Ancestor (A099267) for Civil Service during the Revolution — a tension common among colonial Norwalk families divided by the conflict. In June 1777 he received from his brother Stephen 'the one half of his Negro boy named Dover, now inlisted into the Continental Service,' paying sixty dollars to exempt himself from actual military service.

narrative_blocks.header: Suffered heavy losses when Tryon burned Norwalk, 1779
narrative_blocks.body: William Saint John suffered significant property losses in General Tryon's burning of Norwalk in July 1779, the same raid that destroyed or damaged much of the town. He died February 1, 1800, in his fifty-sixth year — survived by his wife Mary Esther Belden, who would outlive him by fifty years, dying at ninety-seven in 1850.

education.notes: Tutor at Yale at time of death

cross_connections.display_label: His wife's sister married the Farmington minister he succeeded.
Their mothers were sisters — the Fenn family ran through both.
His father-in-law — patriarch of the Fenn family.

narrative_blocks.header: Store at Farmington's most prominent corner
narrative_blocks.body: William Hooker's store stood at the most prominent corner in Farmington — opposite his father's house, at the junction of main street and the road over the stone bridge. He died at 26, leaving only a daughter, Susannah, who was named in her grandfather Rev. Samuel Hooker's will.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Fenn sisters married three prominent men
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Susanna Fenn was one of three Fenn sisters who married into prominent Connecticut families in the same generation. Susanna married him; her sister Martha married Samuel Newton; her sister Mary married William Leete Jr.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at twenty-six with one infant daughter
narrative_blocks.body: He died in 1689 at age 26 — his will dated August 8 with inventory totaling £364-11-06. He left wife Susanna and an infant daughter Susannah; Susanna remarried John Blackleach within a few years.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of Rev. Samuel Hooker, grandson of the founder
narrative_blocks.body: William was a son of Rev. Samuel Hooker of Farmington and a grandson of Thomas Hooker. His store stood across the road from his father's house.

narrative_blocks.header: His widow married the merchant John Blackleach
narrative_blocks.body: After William died in 1689, his widow Susanna Fenn married John Blackleach, a noted Hartford merchant. Their infant daughter Susannah carried the Hooker line forward.

career.notes: Store at the most prominent corner in Farmington, opposite his father's house, at the junction of main street and the road over the stone bridge.

sources.notes: Birth May 4, 1669; daughter of Benjamin Fenn and Susanna Wade

narrative_blocks.header: Her first husband died leaving her pregnant
narrative_blocks.body: William Hooker died in 1689. Susanna gave birth to their daughter Susannah in June 1690 — born into a fatherless household. She then remarried John Blackleach, a noted Hartford merchant.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of Deacon Benjamin Fenn of Milford
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna was a daughter of Benjamin Fenn by his second wife, Susanna Wade, and half-sister to Martha Fenn Newton. She married William Hooker of Farmington in the 1680s.

narrative_blocks.header: She remarried the merchant John Blackleach
narrative_blocks.body: Widowed when William Hooker died in 1689, Susanna married John Blackleach, a prominent Hartford merchant. She died about 1698.

burial.plot_notes: No stone survives. Well-attested tradition places the grave at the south side of Rev. Samuel Whitman's marked grave. "Mr. Whitman like Mr. Hooker, died while the minister of the town, and tradition says he was buried by the side of Mr. Hooker."

sources.notes: John Winthrop Jr., Alchemy and the Creation of New England Culture, 1606-1676 (Homer Babbidge Prize)

narrative_blocks.header: He inherited his father's library — and his calling
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker's will gave his library to John on condition that John pursue ministry; if John faltered, the entire library and manuscripts went to Samuel. John did not become a minister. Samuel received his father's books — and then dedicated his own life to the ministry his father had hoped for.

narrative_blocks.header: Prosecutor in the Hartford witch trials
narrative_blocks.body: In the 1660s he formed a prosecutorial tribunal with Rev. Samuel Stone of Hartford and Rev. Joseph Haynes of Wethersfield. Connecticut tried witches more aggressively than Massachusetts in this decade — three decades before the Salem trials.

narrative_blocks.header: Ordered to King Philip's War, then quietly replaced
narrative_blocks.body: On March 9, 1675-6, the Council ordered Hooker to prepare to march into King Philip's War with Major Treat's army toward Hadley and Northampton. Two days later, the Council quietly replaced him with Rev. Israel Chauncy. No reason was recorded — only the substitution.

narrative_blocks.header: He persuaded Farmington to found a free school
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1685, the town of Farmington voted to give £30 for a schoolmaster for one year — and declared it a free school for the town, almost certainly at Hooker's instigation. It was among the earliest free public schools in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: The 'Fervent Hooker,' who preached from the heart
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Pitkin described him as 'an excellent preacher, his composition good, his address pathetic, warm and engaging.' Hooker told a friend he had three things to do with his sermons before delivering them: write them, commit them to memory, and get them into his heart.

cross_connections.display_label: Five Hooker generations on the same Farmington ground.
Farmington's first pastor, whom Hooker succeeded
His grandfather Tobie Willet sailed with John Talcott on the Lion.
Her husband's Prudden line connects to his wife Mary Willet's family
was his father-in-law; First Mayor of New York City, 1665

quotes.text: Thus we have to this day among us our dead Hooker, yet living in his worthy son Samuel Hooker, an able, faithful, useful minister at Farmington, in the Colony of Connecticut.

education.notes: B.A. 1653. Entered Harvard 1651. Chosen Fellow of the College November 27, 1654.

burial.plot_notes: Near center of burying ground, near East Norwalk railway station

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of New York City's first mayor
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt. Thomas Willet was appointed the first Mayor of New York City in 1665, when the English renamed the Dutch colony. He had previously succeeded Capt. Miles Standish as commander of Plymouth's militia — one of the most storied military commands in early New England. Mary carried this distinguished lineage to Farmington when she married Rev. Samuel Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Three generations of Willet women known for beauty
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's mother was described as possessing 'beauty and majesty almost divine.' A portrait of Mary's daughter Mary Hooker Pierpont survives, and from it contemporaries formed 'the opinion that she was a woman of uncommon beauty.' Her daughter Sarah was 'accounted the most accomplished lady that had ever come to Norwalk.'

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried at 66 — outlived two minister husbands
narrative_blocks.body: After Samuel Hooker's death in 1697, Mary married Reverend Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook at age 66. When he died in 1709 she moved to Norwalk to live with her son-in-law Reverend Stephen Buckingham, where she remained until her death in 1712 at age 74.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her daughter married father and son
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Samuel Hooker died, Mary Willet married Rev. Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook in 1703. Her daughter Sarah was the wife of his son, Rev. Stephen Buckingham, so a mother and daughter had married a father and his son.

cross_connections.display_label: Her grandfather Tobie Willet sailed to New England with John Talcott.
Her father Thomas Willet's widow's first husband was ancestor of H00153's husband

sources.short: Empty Branches on the Family Tree
sources.notes: Blog post with careful analysis; corrects 1630 Mary and John arrival date to likely 1637
Chapter named in honor of Benjamin Fenn

emigration.notes: Likely arrived 1637 with a group from Buckinghamshire — NOT on the 1630 Mary and John as sometimes stated online. First appears in colonial records 1637; seven-year gap makes 1630 arrival implausible. Removed from Dorchester to Milford where he received lot #3 (2 acres 11 rods 3 poles).
emigration.discrepancy_note: Online sources give 1630 / Mary and John. Genealogical blog (Empty Branches) argues compellingly for 1637 Buckinghamshire group. 1637 preferred.

cross_connections.display_label: James Fenn's marriage to Joanna Prudden connected the Fenn-Hooker families.
James Fenn's marriage to Joanna Prudden connected the Fenn-Hooker families.
His daughter Susanna married the Hooker merchant.

notable.primary_url_label: Empty Branches on the Family Tree

narrative_blocks.header: Milford magistrate and a colonial Assistant
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin Fenn was a deacon, magistrate, and militia officer at Milford. He represented the town in the New Haven Colony and served as an Assistant of the colony from 1665 to 1672.

narrative_blocks.header: He took lot number three at Milford
narrative_blocks.body: Born at Stewkley in Buckinghamshire, Fenn came over in the Great Migration and settled at Milford. He held land on both sides of the Atlantic for the rest of his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Two daughters married into the Hooker line
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Martha married Samuel Newton, a grandson of Thomas Hooker; his daughter Susanna married William Hooker of Farmington. Both ties pull the Fenn family into the Hooker tree.

narrative_blocks.header: His will left each grandchild a single ewe
narrative_blocks.body: Fenn's 1672 will divided farms in Milford, New Haven, and Buckinghamshire among his sons. To every one of his grandchildren he left a single ewe sheep.

career.notes: Milford deputy 1653; magistrate 1654-1663; Commissioner of the United Colonies 1661 and 1663.

narrative_blocks.header: Her 1674 will planned a return to England
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin Fenn’s second wife, Susannah Ward, outlived him and in her 1674 will declared her intent to sail home to Worcester, England, where her aged mother was still living.
